playercard.free_to_play: Free to play · No download required

errors.player_not_found_desc: This player does not exist or their profile is private.
errors.generic_desc: Check your connection and try again.

hero.tagline:
A social deduction game of hidden roles, political intrigue,
and impossible trust.

about.label: What Is The Assembly?
about.title: Hidden Roles. Public Stakes.
about.subtitle: A political thriller played out in real time. 5 to 10 players take on secret roles and must deceive, deduce, and outmanoeuvre one another across a series of high-pressure legislative rounds.
about.quote: The old world ended with The Crisis. Now, only The Assembly stands…
about.quote_cta: Will you defend the Civil Charter, or will you build the new State?

factions.label: Choose Your Side
factions.title: Two Factions. One Assembly.
factions.subtitle: Every player is secretly assigned to a faction at the start of each game. The majority will not know who they can trust.

civil.title: Civil
civil.desc: The majority. Isolated in your loyalty. You don't know who your allies are — only that the deck is stacked against you, and that someone at this table is a traitor.

state.title: State
state.desc: The minority. Hidden among the Civil delegates, secretly coordinating. You know your allies. What you need is the right moment.

overseer.title: The Overseer
overseer.desc: A State agent who may not know the other State players. Their election as Chancellor after 3 State directives ends the game immediately.

loop.label: How It Plays
loop.title: One Round. Five Moments.
loop.subtitle: Each round forces every player to participate, declare, and be judged. There is no hiding.
loop.step_1: The President nominates a Chancellor. Who you trust — and who you nominate — reveals everything.
loop.step_2: All players vote simultaneously. Public, inescapable, and open to interpretation.
loop.step_3: The President draws 3 policy cards, discards 1, passes 2 to the Chancellor. One will be enacted.
loop.step_4: Both sides declare what happened in the session. These statements can be lies — blatant, convincing lies.
loop.step_5: Enough State policies and the President wields power — investigations, detentions, executions.

item_1.label: 5–10 Players
item_1.desc: Scale from tight deduction to sprawling deception.

item_2.label: AI Players
item_2.desc: Fill empty seats with AI — each with its own personality.

item_3.label: Title Roles
item_3.desc: One-use powers — Interdictor, Assassin, Broker and more.

item_4.label: Personal Agendas
item_4.desc: 20 hidden objectives independent of your faction.

item_5.label: Crisis Mode
item_5.desc: Optional chaos directives that rewrite the rules mid-game.

item_6.label: Clan System
item_6.desc: Form a clan, set a banner, build a legacy.

item_7.label: Season Ranks
item_7.desc: Climb the ladder. Earn IP. Unlock cosmetics.

item_8.label: Free to Play
item_8.desc: All gameplay is free. Cosmetics are optional.

cta.title: Take Your Seat.
cta.subtitle: Free to play. No downloads. The Assembly awaits.

matchmaking.title: Assemble a Match
matchmaking.subtitle: Join a competitive queue or find a casual room.

browser.empty_create_prompt: Be the first to create one

creator.title: Establish New Assembly
creator.mode_classic_desc: Standard roles/rules. No ELO, reduced points.
creator.mode_crisis_desc: Round-based Event Cards. High risk, high points.
creator.invite_code_hint: An invite code will be generated when the room is created.

practice.title: Solo Practice
practice.subtitle: Vs Artificial Intelligence
practice.difficulty_normal_desc: AIs use standard strategies and deception.
practice.difficulty_elite_desc: AIs are highly deceptive and coordinate effectively.

game.gameOver: G A M E   O V E R

phases.nomination_review: The Assembly is reviewing the nomination.
phases.strategist_deck: Strategist is looking at the deck.
phases.auditor_inspect: Auditor is inspecting the discard pile.
phases.defector_align: The Defector is considering a secret realignment.
phases.quorum_revote: The Quorum is deciding on an emergency re-vote.

dossier.title: Your Secret Dossier
dossier.agenda_bonus: Completing this awards bonus XP and IP equal to a faction win.

interdictor.name: Interdictor
interdictor.desc: Prevents a player from participating in the next vote.

broker.name: Broker
broker.desc: Forces a re-nomination if the current choice is unsatisfactory.

strategist.name: Strategist
strategist.desc: Draws an extra directive card during the legislative session.

auditor.name: Auditor
auditor.desc: Gains access to view the top cards of the discard pile.

assassin.name: Assassin
assassin.desc: Eliminates a player from the game immediately.

handler.name: Handler
handler.desc: Exchanges the positions of the next two Presidents in the order.

defector.name: Defector
defector.desc: Secretly changes their vote result at the end of a session.

archivist.name: Archivist
archivist.desc: Views the top 3 cards of the deck at any time.

quorum.name: Quorum
quorum.desc: Forces a new vote immediately after a failed nomination.

cipher.name: Cipher
cipher.desc: Sends an anonymous private message to any player.

action_bar.censure_debate: The Assembly is debating a Censure Motion.
action_bar.snap_volunteers: Volunteers are stepping forward for the Snap Election.
action_bar.investigating: President is investigating a player's loyalty.
action_bar.calling_special: President is calling a Special Election.
action_bar.peeking: President is reviewing the top three policies.
action_bar.deciding: President is deciding on an executive action.
action_bar.nominate_prompt: Tap a player on the board to nominate them as Chancellor.
action_bar.nominate_watch: Watch the nomination — it reveals who the President trusts.
action_bar.detained_cant_vote: You are detained and cannot vote this round.
action_bar.voting_prompt: Vote AYE to support this government or NAY to block it.
action_bar.discard_prompt: Discard one policy card. The other two go to the Chancellor.
action_bar.pass_watch: The President is choosing which policy to pass to the Chancellor.
action_bar.declaration_hint: Declarations tell the table what was drawn and passed. They may be lies.
action_bar.enact_prompt: Enact one policy. You may propose a Veto if 5+ State directives are enacted.
action_bar.enact_watch: The Chancellor is choosing which policy to enact.
action_bar.investigate_prompt: Select a player to investigate their party loyalty.
action_bar.special_prompt: Select a player to be the next President.
action_bar.peek_prompt: Review the top 3 cards of the deck.
action_bar.must_use_power: You must use your executive power.
action_bar.pres_must_use: The President must use their executive power.
action_bar.lobby_ready_prompt: Press Ready Up when you're ready to start. The game begins when all players are ready.
action_bar.censure_select: Select a player on their card to censure (exclude from next nomination)
action_bar.snap_volunteer_prompt: Any player may volunteer to be the next President.
action_bar.prediction_reward: 50 IP will be credited if your faction prevails.
action_bar.title_strategist_prompt: Use Strategist power to draw an extra policy?
action_bar.title_broker_prompt: Use Broker power to force a re-nomination?
action_bar.title_handler_prompt: Use Handler power to swap the next two players in the presidential order?
action_bar.title_auditor_prompt: Use Auditor power to peek at the discard pile?
action_bar.title_archivist_prompt: Use Archivist power to peek at the discard pile?
action_bar.title_defector_prompt: Defect? Secretly flip your vote to the opposite result?
action_bar.title_quorum_prompt: Use Quorum power to call for an emergency re-vote?
action_bar.title_assassin_prompt: Select a player to execute on their card, or skip.
action_bar.title_interdictor_prompt: Select a player to detain on their card, or skip.

chanCivilStateEnacted.0: I was handed two State directives. The President owes the Assembly an explanation.
chanCivilStateEnacted.1: There was no Civil directive in what I received. Direct your questions to the President.
chanCivilStateEnacted.2: I enacted what I was given. The President controls the discard.
chanCivilStateEnacted.3: The President passed me a dead hand. I had no choice.
chanCivilStateEnacted.4: Ask the President why they only passed State directives to me.
chanCivilStateEnacted.5: My hands were tied by the President's choice.
chanCivilStateEnacted.6: I am merely the instrument of the President's will.
chanCivilStateEnacted.7: The President's discard pile tells the real story.
chanCivilStateEnacted.8: Don't blame me for the President's incompetence.
chanCivilStateEnacted.9: The President gave me no other option.
chanCivilStateEnacted.10: I only saw State directives. The President is hiding something.
chanCivilStateEnacted.11: The President's choices left me with no alternative.
chanCivilStateEnacted.12: Check the discard pile. The President didn't give me a choice.
chanCivilStateEnacted.13: I'm as disappointed as you are, but the President passed me two State cards.
chanCivilStateEnacted.14: The President is the one who filtered the draw. Ask them.
chanCivilStateEnacted.15: I had to enact a State directive because that's all I received.
chanCivilStateEnacted.16: The President's hand was full of State, apparently.
chanCivilStateEnacted.17: I'm not the one who discarded the Civil card. The President is.
chanCivilStateEnacted.18: The President's selection was purely State-oriented.
chanCivilStateEnacted.19: I am just the messenger. The President wrote the message.
chanCivilStateEnacted.20: The President's discard was more telling than their words.
chanCivilStateEnacted.21: A strictly State hand was passed to me. The President holds the cards.
chanCivilStateEnacted.22: I'm not the one filtering the directives. Ask the President.
chanCivilStateEnacted.23: The President's choices left me with a single path.
chanCivilStateEnacted.24: I performed my duty with the limited options the President provided.
chanCivilStateEnacted.25: The President's narrative doesn't match the hand I received.
chanCivilStateEnacted.26: I enacted a State directive because the President gave me no Civil alternative.
chanCivilStateEnacted.27: The draw was supposedly all State—at least that is what the President says.
chanCivilStateEnacted.28: The President's discard at the Secretariat was likely where our hope died.
chanCivilStateEnacted.29: I am bound by the Presidency's selection, which was entirely State.
chanCivilStateEnacted.30: The President is the filter; I am just the output. Blame the filter.
chanCivilStateEnacted.31: A dead hand from the Presidency. I did what I had to.
chanCivilStateEnacted.32: The President's choices were... restrictive, to say the least.
chanCivilStateEnacted.33: I was handed a poisoned chalice by the President.

chanStateStateEnacted.0: The draw was against us. This is the deck's doing, not mine.
chanStateStateEnacted.1: I had no viable alternative. The President can verify.
chanStateStateEnacted.2: Sometimes the Secretariat decides for us.
chanStateStateEnacted.3: A difficult hand, but we must proceed.
chanStateStateEnacted.4: The odds were not in our favor this round.
chanStateStateEnacted.5: The deck is clearly rigged against the Charter.
chanStateStateEnacted.6: I did what I had to do for the State.
chanStateStateEnacted.7: The cards were not in our favor.
chanStateStateEnacted.8: Blame the deck, not the player.
chanStateStateEnacted.9: The State demands sacrifice, and this was it.
chanStateStateEnacted.10: The draw was unfortunate. We must adapt.
chanStateStateEnacted.11: The Secretariat's will is sometimes difficult to accept.
chanStateStateEnacted.12: I acted in the best interest of the current situation.
chanStateStateEnacted.13: The deck's randomness is a challenge for us all.
chanStateStateEnacted.14: Sometimes the State's path is the only one open.
chanStateStateEnacted.15: I followed the logic of the cards I was dealt.
chanStateStateEnacted.16: The draw was heavily weighted towards the State.
chanStateStateEnacted.17: We must remain united despite this setback.
chanStateStateEnacted.18: The cards dictated the outcome this time.
chanStateStateEnacted.19: I am committed to the Assembly, even when the draw is hard.
chanStateStateEnacted.20: The State's tide is rising. We are merely floating on the wreckage of the draw.
chanStateStateEnacted.21: The probability was against us. Even the Secretariat has bad days.
chanStateStateEnacted.22: A forced enactment of the State's will. We must endure.
chanStateStateEnacted.23: The dice of destiny rolled for the State this time.
chanStateStateEnacted.24: Don't read too much into a single draw. The deck is fickle.
chanStateStateEnacted.25: The State's influence is woven into the very fabric of this deck.
chanStateStateEnacted.26: A mandatory enactment for the current regime. Nothing more.
chanStateStateEnacted.27: The Chancellor's hand is often tied by the Secretariat's randomness.
chanStateStateEnacted.28: The cards were weighted. We are all victims of the draw.
chanStateStateEnacted.29: Sometimes the path of the State is the only path paved.
chanStateStateEnacted.30: The draw was a statistical anomaly that favored our rivals.
chanStateStateEnacted.31: I hold no malice, only the cards the President deemed fit.
chanStateStateEnacted.32: The Secretariat breathes State directives when we need them least.
chanStateStateEnacted.33: A forced play in a game of limited choices.

presCivilStateEnacted.0: I passed a Civil directive. The Chancellor exercised their own discretion.
presCivilStateEnacted.1: There was a Civil option in that hand. Make of that what you will.
presCivilStateEnacted.2: I gave them a choice. They chose the State.
presCivilStateEnacted.3: The Chancellor is responsible for that enactment. I passed them a Civil option.
presCivilStateEnacted.4: I am surprised by the Chancellor's decision. I sent them a Civil directive.
presCivilStateEnacted.5: The Chancellor clearly has their own agenda.
presCivilStateEnacted.6: I did my part. The Chancellor failed the Assembly.
presCivilStateEnacted.7: I offered a Civil path. They rejected it.
presCivilStateEnacted.8: The Chancellor's betrayal is their own.
presCivilStateEnacted.9: I cannot control the Chancellor's actions.
presCivilStateEnacted.10: I passed a Civil card. Why did the Chancellor choose State?
presCivilStateEnacted.11: The Chancellor had a choice. They chose poorly.
presCivilStateEnacted.12: I am questioning the Chancellor's loyalty after that move.
presCivilStateEnacted.13: I gave them the option to be Civil. They declined.
presCivilStateEnacted.14: The Chancellor's enactment does not reflect the hand I passed.
presCivilStateEnacted.15: I am deeply concerned by the Chancellor's decision.
presCivilStateEnacted.16: The Chancellor had a Civil directive in their hand.
presCivilStateEnacted.17: I did my part to keep us Civil. The Chancellor did not.
presCivilStateEnacted.18: The Chancellor's true colors are showing.
presCivilStateEnacted.19: I cannot answer for the Chancellor's betrayal.
presCivilStateEnacted.20: I sent them a choice of futures. They chose a darker one.
presCivilStateEnacted.21: The Chancellor's enactment is a direct slap to the Presidency.
presCivilStateEnacted.22: I am shocked. The Chancellor had a Civil directive in their grasp.
presCivilStateEnacted.23: The Chancellor has some explaining to do to the Assembly.
presCivilStateEnacted.24: I did my part to preserve the Charter. The Chancellor did not.
presCivilStateEnacted.25: The Chancellor's actions are increasingly erratic—or perhaps calculated.
presCivilStateEnacted.26: I passed a Civil card. The Chancellor's choice is their own burden.
presCivilStateEnacted.27: Why would the Chancellor do this? I gave them a clear Civil path.
presCivilStateEnacted.28: The Chancellor is playing a dangerous game with the State.
presCivilStateEnacted.29: I provided the option to be Civil. The Chancellor rejected it.
presCivilStateEnacted.30: The Chancellor's loyalty to the Charter is officially in question.
presCivilStateEnacted.31: I am as confused as the rest of you. I passed them a Civil card.
presCivilStateEnacted.32: The Chancellor had a fork in the road. they chose the State path.
presCivilStateEnacted.33: I cannot stand by the Chancellor's decision. It wasn't my hand.

presStateStateEnacted.0: Three State draws. Neither of us had a choice.
presStateStateEnacted.1: The deck was working against the Charter this round.
presStateStateEnacted.2: I passed on what I could. The hand was compromised.
presStateStateEnacted.3: The Secretariat is testing us. No Civil cards in that draw.
presStateStateEnacted.4: A pure State draw. My hands were tied.
presStateStateEnacted.5: The deck is not our friend today.
presStateStateEnacted.6: I had no Civil cards to pass. Believe me.
presStateStateEnacted.7: The State's influence is strong in this deck.
presStateStateEnacted.8: I am just as frustrated as you are.
presStateStateEnacted.9: The cards were stacked against us from the start.
presStateStateEnacted.10: A triple State draw. There was nothing I could do.
presStateStateEnacted.11: The deck is clearly favoring the State right now.
presStateStateEnacted.12: I'm just as stuck as you are with these cards.
presStateStateEnacted.13: The Secretariat is pushing us towards the State.
presStateStateEnacted.14: No Civil cards were available in that draw.
presStateStateEnacted.15: The draw was a complete washout for the Charter.
presStateStateEnacted.16: I had zero Civil options to pass along.
presStateStateEnacted.17: The deck is being very uncooperative today.
presStateStateEnacted.18: We are at the mercy of the draw sometimes.
presStateStateEnacted.19: The State's influence is strong in this deck.
presStateStateEnacted.20: Three State cards stared back at me. Even the Secretariat was silent.
presStateStateEnacted.21: A pure State hand. No choice for me, no choice for the Chancellor.
presStateStateEnacted.22: The odds finally caught up with us. Not a single Civil card in sight.
presStateStateEnacted.23: The deck's bias towards the State was total this round.
presStateStateEnacted.24: I passed on the State because there was nothing else to pass.
presStateStateEnacted.25: A total washout of Civil directives. We are at the mercy of the draw.
presStateStateEnacted.26: The draw was a symphony of State directives. I am just the conductor.
presStateStateEnacted.27: Zero options for the Charter. My hands were tied by the cards.
presStateStateEnacted.28: The Secretariat is having a dark round. Triple State cards.
presStateStateEnacted.29: I looked for a Civil card. I found none. The draw was absolute.
presStateStateEnacted.30: The draw was entirely comprised of State directives. Unfortunate.
presStateStateEnacted.31: Not a single lick of Civil logic in that hand. All State.
presStateStateEnacted.32: The Secretariat's draw was a monochrome of State directives.
presStateStateEnacted.33: I am as disappointed as anyone. The deck is definitely against us.

governmentFailed.0: A wasted session. We cannot keep stalling the Secretariat.
governmentFailed.1: Deadlock serves no one here. The Crisis does not wait.
governmentFailed.2: We need to form a functional government. Now.
governmentFailed.3: The Assembly is divided. This is dangerous.
governmentFailed.4: Another failed vote. The State grows stronger with every delay.
governmentFailed.5: We are running out of time.
governmentFailed.7: We must come to an agreement.
governmentFailed.10: We are paralyzed. This is exactly what the State wants.
governmentFailed.11: Every failed vote is a victory for our enemies.
governmentFailed.12: The Assembly's indecision is our greatest weakness.
governmentFailed.13: We must find common ground or face the consequences.
governmentFailed.14: This obstructionism is destroying the Charter.
governmentFailed.15: The Crisis is deepening while we argue.
governmentFailed.16: We cannot afford another round of failure.
governmentFailed.17: The State is laughing at our division.
governmentFailed.19: The Assembly is spinning its wheels.
governmentFailed.20: Every 'Nay' vote is a pulse of life for the State's ambitions.
governmentFailed.21: We are bickering while the Charter burns. Form a government!
governmentFailed.22: This indecision is a death sentence for the Assembly.
governmentFailed.23: The Secretariat despises a vacuum. We must fill it with leadership.
governmentFailed.24: Are we here to debate or to enact the Charter? Form a government.
governmentFailed.25: The State thrives in the shadows of our disagreement.
governmentFailed.26: Another failed attempt. We are becoming as weak as our enemies.
governmentFailed.27: The Assembly's lack of unity is its own undoing.
governmentFailed.28: We are handing the State the upper hand through our own division.
governmentFailed.29: A government of straw is better than no government at all.
governmentFailed.30: The Crisis only intensifies while we stall. Make a choice!
governmentFailed.31: The Secretariat is growing impatient with our paralysis.
governmentFailed.32: Obstructionism is a slow poison for the Charter.
governmentFailed.33: We are arguing over titles while the building burns around us.

investigateCivil.0: The investigation confirms their alignment, {{name}} is Civil.
investigateCivil.1: Nothing of concern found. {{name}} is Charter-loyal.
investigateCivil.2: {{name}} appears to stand with the Assembly.
investigateCivil.3: I've cleared {{name}}. They are one of us.
investigateCivil.4: My findings on {{name}} are positive. They support the Charter.
investigateCivil.7: The investigation was successful. {{name}} is Civil.
investigateCivil.10: I've looked into {{name}} and found a loyal citizen.
investigateCivil.11: Nothing of concern found. {{name}} is Charter-loyal.
investigateCivil.12: My report on {{name}} is entirely positive.
investigateCivil.13: I'm happy to report that {{name}} is Civil.
investigateCivil.14: {{name}} has my full confidence after this investigation.
investigateCivil.16: I've verified that {{name}} is on our side.

banter.2: One wrong move and the Charter falls.
banter.3: Trust is a rare commodity in these times.
banter.4: Let's focus on the directives. The Assembly depends on us.

banter.11: We are the last line of defense for the Charter.
banter.12: Every decision we make echoes in the Secretariat.
banter.13: The State's reach is long, but our resolve is strong.

banter.33: We are the architects of the future, or the gravediggers of the past.

cipherDispatch.0: A contact in the Secretariat suggests you are not what you seem.
cipherDispatch.1: The data stream is clear: stay the course and the Charter will prevail.
cipherDispatch.2: Vigilance is required. The shadows are deepening around the Assembly.
cipherDispatch.3: Do not trust the current administration. They are compromised.
cipherDispatch.4: The State's influence is localized. Look closer at your allies.
cipherDispatch.5: A coded message for you: the outcome of the next session is critical.

declaration.title: Directive Declaration
declaration.subtitle: What will you declare?
declaration.hint: You may report truthfully or mislead the Assembly.

banners.civil_desc: The civil forces have defended the assembly.
banners.state_desc: The state has successfully infiltrated the assembly.

debrief.secretariat_fallen: The Secretariat has fallen to the State.

plays.chaos_one: A chaos directive was forced upon the Assembly
plays.veto_one: The government invoked their veto power
plays.deadlock: Both factions held the Assembly in a knife-edge deadlock

premise.title: The Assembly
premise.subtitle: A game of hidden loyalty
premise.p1: The world ended with {{crisis}}. What remains is The Assembly — a council of delegates trying to govern the ruins.
premise.p2: But not everyone wants to restore order. Some want to build a new State — and one among them, the {{overseer}}, is waiting to seize total power.
premise.label: The Assembly is
premise.desc: A social deduction game for 5–10 players. You are given a secret role. Use it, lie with it, or die by it.

civil.name: Civil
civil.flavor: Defend the Charter. The Crisis must not consume the Secretariat.

state.name: State
state.flavor: Enact State directives. Elevate the Overseer to power.

overseer.name: The Overseer
overseer.flavor: Ascend to the Chancellorship. State Supremacy awaits.

loop.title: The Game Loop
loop.subtitle: Each round follows the same sequence

step_1.label: Presidential Nomination
step_1.desc: The current President nominates a Chancellor candidate. The presidential role rotates each round.

step_2.label: Assembly Vote
step_2.desc: All living players vote AYE or NAY on the proposed government. Majority AYE passes it.

step_3.label: Legislative Session
step_3.desc: The President draws 3 policy cards, discards 1, and passes 2 to the Chancellor. The Chancellor enacts one.

step_4.label: Declarations
step_4.desc: Both President and Chancellor publicly declare what cards they saw and passed. This is how trust is built — or broken.

loop.chaos_desc: If 3 elections fail in a row, a {{chaos}} is drawn automatically and enacted without a vote.

voting.title: Voting & Declarations
voting.subtitle: The heart of social deduction
voting.p1: Votes are {{simultaneous}} until revealed. You can't see how others voted before you cast yours.
voting.decl_p1: After a policy is enacted, the President and Chancellor publicly declare what cards they drew and passed. These claims can be {{true_or_false}} — the table must decide who to believe.
voting.decl_hint: Contradictions between the President's and Chancellor's declarations are your most important clue.

executive.title: Executive Actions
executive.subtitle: Power grows as State advances
executive.p1: Each time a State directive is enacted, the President may unlock a special executive power. These grow more dangerous as State advances.

peek.name: Policy Peek
peek.desc: See the top 3 cards in the draw pile.

investigate.name: Investigate
investigate.desc: Learn whether a player is Civil or State (not who specifically).

special.name: Special Election
special.desc: Choose who will be the next President, skipping normal rotation.

execution.name: Execution
execution.desc: Permanently eliminate a player. If it's the Overseer, Civil wins instantly.

executive.footer: Executive actions are given to the current President and must be used before the next round begins.

assassin.name: Assassin
assassin.desc: Eliminate a player from the game.

strategist.name: Strategist
strategist.desc: Draw an extra policy (4 total) when you are President.

broker.name: Broker
broker.desc: Force a re-nomination if the current one is unfavorable.

handler.name: Handler
handler.desc: Swap the next two players in the presidential order.

auditor.name: Auditor
auditor.desc: Peek at the last 3 discarded policies after a legislative session.

interdictor.name: Interdictor
interdictor.desc: Detain a player for one round.

archivist.name: Archivist
archivist.desc: Inspect the entire Discard Pile once per game.

defector.name: Defector
defector.desc: Secretly flip your vote after the initial reveal to change the outcome.

quorum.name: Quorum
quorum.desc: Call for an emergency re-vote during high-tension failed elections.

cipher.name: Cipher
cipher.desc: Send an anonymous dispatch to the room during the legislative phase.

agendas.title: Personal Agendas
agendas.subtitle: Your hidden individual goal
agendas.p1: Every player is secretly assigned a {{agenda}} — a hidden objective that earns bonus XP and IP if completed, regardless of whether your faction wins or loses.
examples: Vote Nay in at least 3 rounds
Be nominated as Chancellor at least twice
The game must end before round (player count + 3)
No chaos policy is enacted during the game
agendas.footer: Completing your agenda awards {{bonus}} and bonus IP — the same as winning with your faction. Win {{and}} complete your agenda for double rewards.
agendas.footer_sub: Your agenda is visible in your Dossier at all times. Others can't see it.

civil.heading: Civil players: trust nobody
civil.body: You outnumber State but you don't know who they are. Contradictory declarations, suspicious nominations, and patterns in voting are your only clues.

state.heading: State players: coordinate carefully
state.body: You know your allies but can't communicate openly. Coordinate through nominations, declarations, and subtle voting patterns — without being obvious.

decl.heading: Declarations are everything
decl.body: After every legislative session, both President and Chancellor declare what they saw. Cross-referencing these claims is how you detect lies.

dossier.heading: Watch your Dossier
dossier.body: Your role, title ability, and personal agenda are always visible in your Dossier — tap the icon in the header during a game.

dossier.title: Confidential Dossier
dossier.agenda_bonus: Completing this grants bonus XP and IP.

banners.state_desc: The Secretariat has fallen to the State.
banners.abort_desc: The Assembly has collapsed due to a disconnection.

match_replay.title: Match Replay
match_replay.subtitle: Post-Match Reconstruction
match_replay.no_history: No history recorded for this match
match_replay.fail_veto: Both parties agreed to exercise veto power.
match_replay.fail_default: The Assembly failed to confirm this coalition.
match_replay.footer_info: Replay uses decrypted archival data. Use the timeline above to navigate through match milestones.

overview.p1: The Assembly is a {{social_deduction_game}} for 5–10 players. Each player is secretly assigned a faction — Civil or State — and must work with or against the others to achieve their faction's win condition.
overview.crisis_desc: A chaotic variant featuring {{event_cards}}. Every round, a random event is drawn that can freeze the election tracker, blackout the chat, or force immediate snap elections.
overview.deck_desc: The deck contains {{civil_count}} and {{state_count}} policy cards. The deck is stacked against Civil — deduction and coordination are essential.
overview.tracker_desc: If three elections fail in a row, a {{chaos_policy}} is drawn from the top of the deck and enacted automatically without a vote.

civil.label: Civil
civil.desc: The majority. You don't know who your allies are. Use declarations, voting patterns, and nominations to find the State agents and stop them.

state.label: State
state.desc: The minority. You know who your allies are. Secretly coordinate to enact State directives or get the Overseer elected as Chancellor.

overseer.label: The Overseer
overseer.desc: A State agent who doesn't know the other State players. Wins with State. If elected Chancellor after 3 State directives are enacted, State wins immediately.
overseer.distribution_hint: At 5–6 players, the Overseer knows the other State agents.

step_1.label: Nomination
step_1.desc: The current President nominates a Chancellor. The same player cannot serve as Chancellor in consecutive rounds (or as President in larger games).

step_2.label: Vote
step_2.desc: All living players simultaneously vote AYE or NAY. A strict majority of AYE passes the government. Ties and equal splits fail.

step_3.label: Legislative Session
step_3.desc: President draws 3 cards, discards 1 face-down, passes 2 to Chancellor. Chancellor enacts 1, discards the other. Neither can see what the other discarded.

step_4.label: Declarations
step_4.desc: President declares how many Civil/State they drew and passed. Chancellor declares how many they received and enacted. These claims can be lies.

step_5.label: Executive Action
step_5.desc: If a State directive was enacted and the player count/directive count triggers a power, the President must use it before the round ends.

titles_tab.intro: Title Roles are assigned randomly at game start. Each is single-use and fires at a specific point in the round. Check your Dossier to see if you have one.

interdictor.name: Interdictor
interdictor.desc: Before nomination: detain a player for this round. They cannot vote or be nominated as Chancellor. Once used.

broker.name: Broker
broker.desc: Review the Chancellor nomination and veto it, forcing the President to nominate again. Once used.

strategist.name: Strategist
strategist.desc: When you're President, draw 4 policy cards instead of 3, then discard 2. Once used.

auditor.name: Auditor
auditor.desc: Peek at the last 3 cards in the discard pile. Useful for tracking what policies are gone. Once used.

assassin.name: Assassin
assassin.desc: When you're President, secretly execute any living player after the round ends. Once used.

handler.name: Handler
handler.desc: Swap the next two players in the presidential rotation. The second becomes President before the first. Once used.

defector.name: Defector
defector.desc: Secretly change your vote after the initial reveal but before the result is finalized. This can flip a 5-5 tie into a 6-4 pass. Once used.

archivist.name: Archivist
archivist.desc: Deep inspection: peek at the top 3 cards of the Draw Pile before the next round begins. Once used.

quorum.name: Quorum
quorum.desc: Emergency session: force an immediate re-vote on the last failed government, bypassing the next President. Once used.

cipher.name: Cipher
cipher.desc: Send a one-way encrypted message to any player. Only they can see the text you send. Once used.

agendas_tab.p1: Every player receives a hidden {{personal_agenda}} at the start of each game. Completing it earns {{xp_bonus}} — equal to winning with your faction — regardless of the game result.

chaos_agent.name: Chaos Agent
chaos_agent.desc: Trigger a chaos policy at least once

dove.name: The Dove
dove.desc: Vote Aye in every eligible round

hawk.name: The Hawk
hawk.desc: Vote Nay in at least 3 rounds

stonewalled.name: Stonewalled
stonewalled.desc: Vote Nay on 2 governments that fail

deadlock.name: Deadlock
deadlock.desc: Be in at least 1 failed government

veteran.name: The Veteran
veteran.desc: Serve as Chancellor at least once

unity.name: Unity
unity.desc: No chaos policy during the game

clean_sweep.name: Clean Sweep
clean_sweep.desc: Enact a Civil directive as Chancellor

productive.name: Productive Session
productive.desc: More than half of rounds enact a policy

close_race.name: Close Race
close_race.desc: Tracks within 2 directives at game end

swing_vote.name: The Swing Vote
swing_vote.desc: Be in a government that passes by 1 vote

civil.label: For Civil Players
tips: Cross-reference declarations: if President says they passed 1C/1S but Chancellor says they received 2S, someone is lying.
Track the voting record. Consistent Nay voting without explanation is suspicious.
Be careful with executive actions — investigate based on evidence, not gut feeling.
The deck is State-heavy. Some State policies being enacted doesn't prove anyone is guilty.

state.label: For State Players
tips: Coordinate declarations carefully. Contradicting your State ally's claim is a giveaway.
Nominate your State allies as Chancellor when possible — but not so obviously it looks suspicious.
Blame bad hands. "I only had State cards" is plausible because the deck is State-heavy.
The Overseer should stay quiet and unremarkable until the moment is right.

general.label: General
tips: Use the chat and declarations together. What players say matters.
Your personal agenda might conflict with your faction's best play. That tension is intentional.
The round history button shows every government, every vote, and every declaration.

voting.detained: You are detained and cannot vote this round

civil.name: Civil
civil.flavor: Protect the Charter. Trust no one.

state.name: State
state.flavor: Implement the State's vision. Identify your allies.

overseer.name: Overseer
overseer.flavor: Sabotage the Assembly from within. Become the Chancellor.

round_history.title: Archive: Floor History
